species: Cascadia Subduction Zone -- Juan de Fuca Plate tectonic acoustic emission
sound_type: Very low infrasonic acoustic emission: (1) Episodic Tremor and Slip (ETS) non-volcanic tremor at 1-10 Hz, occurring ~every 14 months along the full CSZ margin; (2) deep Pacific Ocean ambient sound: microseismic noise (0.05-0.5 Hz), thermohaline circulation pressure variations; (3) Juan de Fuca Ridge hydrothermal vent acoustics (Axial Seamount, ~300 km offshore WA). FIRST GEOPHYSICAL ENTRY IN SPS ENGINE. All signals are infrasonic (below 20 Hz) and detectable by seafloor hydrophone networks (NEPTUNE Canada, Ocean Observatories Initiative).
ecosystem: Deep Pacific Ocean / Cascadia Subduction Zone -- the Juan de Fuca Plate subduction boundary from northern California through Oregon, Washington, and British Columbia. Depth: ocean floor (2,500-3,000 m at the subduction boundary) through the 25-45 km depth of the ETS transition zone. Geographic range: 1,100 km along-strike. The acoustic signals propagate through both the water column and the rock column.
season: Geological -- continuous (not seasonal). ETS events recur every ~14 months with sub-events more frequent.
milestone: FIRST GEOPHYSICAL ENTRY IN SPS ENGINE. E=5. The Cascadia Subduction Zone is the largest geological feature documented in the engine (1,100 km fault, ~250,000 km2 Juan de Fuca Plate). Last full-margin rupture: January 26, 1700 (M9.0, documented by Japanese orphan tsunami, PNW tree rings, Native oral traditions, turbidite deposits). Current status: locked zone accumulating stress. Return interval: 300-500 years. The Pacific Northwest is within the return window.

coordinates.description: Cascadia Subduction Zone: offshore fault runs from northern California (~40°N) through Oregon and Washington to southern British Columbia (~50°N). ETS transition zone: beneath Puget Sound basin and western Washington/Oregon, 25-45 km depth. Juan de Fuca Ridge (spreading center): ~300-400 km offshore Washington coast. Axial Seamount (most monitored offshore volcano): 46°N, 130°W.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: ETS non-volcanic tremor: continuous burst-type seismic signal at 1-10 Hz produced by fluid migration and micro-cracking in the subduction zone transition zone during slow-slip events. Duration: days to weeks per ETS episode. Detectable at seafloor hydrophone stations as low-frequency pressure fluctuations distinct from biological sources. Deep ocean ambient: microseismic double-frequency peak (0.1-0.5 Hz) from ocean surface wave interaction; thermohaline circulation (<0.01 Hz).
acoustic_data.mechanics: Subduction zone acoustic generation: (1) ETS tremor is generated by fluid-mediated shear fracturing along the plate interface; similar mechanism to volcanic tremor but not associated with volcanic activity. (2) Deep ocean microseism: ocean surface waves interact to generate secondary pressure waves that propagate to the seafloor. (3) Hydrothermal vents: superheated water (300-400°C) expelled through fractures produces acoustic signals as it contacts cold seawater. All three mechanisms produce detectable infrasonic signals at the seafloor.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: Primary geological signal: 0.01-10 Hz (infrasonic, below human hearing threshold of 20 Hz). ETS tremor peak: 1-10 Hz. Microseismic noise: 0.05-0.5 Hz (primary) and 0.1-1.0 Hz (secondary). Thermohaline: <0.01 Hz. Converted to audio at 100x speed-up: ETS tremor occupies 100 Hz-1 kHz, sounds like sustained rain or distant surf with intermittent burst-type signals.
acoustic_data.e5_calibration: E=5: Four factors: (1) physically real acoustic emission documented by instrument (NEPTUNE Canada, OOI): not hypothetical; (2) geological significance: the largest geological feature in the PNW ecosystem, the source of the 1700 M9.0 megathrust, the engine's most consequential non-biological acoustic source; (3) perceptual near-absence: infrasonic frequencies are below human hearing threshold at surface; the signal is 'heard' only by instrument or by proximity to the seafloor; (4) scale vs. intensity distinction: the geological system is enormous (E=10+ by absolute energy scale) but the continuous ambient signal is below E=5 biological intensity in the human-perceptible range. E=5 is the correct mid-scale anchor for the geological baseline.

nodes.title: First Geophysical Entry in SPS Engine
nodes.description: The CSZ deep current entry is the engine's first non-biological acoustic entry. All previous 317 SPS entries have documented biological sound sources (wildlife vocalizations, ocean weather, etc.). The CSZ entry expands the engine's scope to include geological acoustic processes: the infrasonic baseline beneath all biological acoustic events in the PNW.

nodes.title: Episodic Tremor and Slip -- The 14-Month Geological Clock
nodes.description: ETS recurs every ~14 months along the full CSZ margin. Non-volcanic tremor (1-10 Hz) accompanies slow-slip events in the transition zone. The regularity is one of the most precisely timed geological phenomena in the PNW. ETS is 'the geological clock of Cascadia' -- the engine's only entry with a measurable recurrence interval shorter than the geological epoch.

nodes.title: The 1700 M9.0 -- What Degree 318 Is the Prelude To
nodes.description: January 26, 1700, approximately 9 PM Pacific: M9.0 full-margin rupture, 1,000 km along-strike, 10-30m run-up on PNW coast, Japan tsunami 10 hours later. Documented by Japanese orphan tsunami records, PNW tree rings, Native oral traditions, turbidite deposits. Return interval: 300-500 years. The engine documents the between-event baseline (degree 318); the megathrust itself would be above the scale.

nodes.title: Scale vs. Intensity -- Why the Largest Object is E=5
nodes.description: The CSZ is the largest object the SPS engine has documented by physical scale. The E-value system measures perceptual acoustic intensity in the environment, not absolute physical scale. The CSZ's continuous ambient signal is infrasonic and near-imperceptible at the surface. E=5 reflects: real + significant + sub-threshold perceptually. The distinction between scale and intensity is documented here as an explicit calibration principle.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=5 = E=12. BELOW-THE-SURFACE PAIRING: Candlebox below the critical grunge canon (4M copies, acoustically present, narratively invisible) + CSZ below the Pacific floor (physically vast, acoustically present in infrasonic range, surface-invisible). Both systems are operating below the visible surface of their contexts.

Ocean surface (storm, E=9) vs. ocean floor (CSZ baseline, E=5). The full Pacific water column documented: surface weather acoustic events at E=9 and geological floor acoustic events at E=5. 4 units of E-difference between the surface and the bottom.

Degree 318 = between-event baseline. The 1700 M9.0 (or future equivalent) = the event itself. The megathrust future entry would require a special calibration beyond E=9 -- the engine's maximum biological scale is not adequate for a full-margin M9 rupture. Documented as open for future treatment.
